section.title: Purity of the Camp
section.sectionRationale: Before Israel departs from Sinai, the camp itself must be kept ceremonially and morally pure: those defiled by disease or death are sent outside, wrongs against a neighbor must be confessed and repaid, and a suspected marital unfaithfulness is entrusted to God alone through a solemn ritual, together showing that the LORD who dwells in the midst of the camp demands purity in body, in restitution, and in the hidden places of the heart.
section.thesis: Because the LORD Himself dwells among His people, the camp must be kept clean from defilement, wrongs must be confessed and made right, and even secret unfaithfulness that no human court can prove is entrusted to God's own righteous judgment, showing that holiness reaches into what is hidden as well as what is seen.
section.frameNote: Four movements: sending the ceremonially unclean outside the camp; confession and restitution for wrongs done to another; the ritual law of jealousy for a wife suspected in secret; and the law's closing summary.

units.label: Sending out the unclean
units.note: so the camp where God dwells is not defiled

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Command the Israelites to send away from the camp anyone with a skin disease, anyone who has a bodily discharge, and anyone who is defiled by a dead body.
You must send away male and female alike; send them outside the camp so they will not defile their camp, where I dwell among them.”
So the Israelites did this, sending such people outside the camp. They did just as the LORD had instructed Moses.

units.label: Confession and restitution
units.note: repaying a wrong, plus a fifth

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell the Israelites that when a man or woman acts {{unfaithful|unfaithfully}} against the LORD by committing any sin against another, that person is guilty
and must confess the sin he has committed. He must make full restitution, add a fifth to its value, and give all this to the one he has wronged.

But if the man has no relative to whom restitution can be made for the wrong, the restitution belongs to the LORD and must be given to the priest along with the ram of atonement, by which the atonement is made for him.
Every sacred contribution the Israelites bring to the priest shall belong to him.
Each man's sacred gifts are his own, but whatever he gives to the priest will belong to the priest.”

units.label: The law of jealousy: the ritual
units.note: God alone as judge of what cannot be proven

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them that if any man's wife goes astray and is unfaithful to him
by sleeping with another man, and it is concealed from her husband and her impurity is undetected, since there is no witness against her and she was not caught in the act,

and if a feeling of {{jealousy|jealousy}} comes over her husband and he suspects his wife who has defiled herself, or if a feeling of jealousy comes over him and he suspects her even though she has not defiled herself,

then he is to bring his wife to the priest. 
He must also bring for her an offering of a tenth of an ephah of barley flour. He is not to pour oil over it or put frankincense on it, because it is a grain offering for jealousy, an offering of memorial as a reminder of iniquity.
The priest is to bring the wife forward and have her stand before the LORD.
Then he is to take some holy water in a clay jar and put some of the dust from the tabernacle floor into the water.

After the priest has the woman stand before the LORD, he is to let down her hair and place in her hands the grain offering of memorial, which is the grain offering for jealousy. The priest is to hold the bitter water that brings a curse.

And he is to put the woman under oath and say to her, ‘If no other man has slept with you and you have not gone astray and become defiled while under your husband's authority, may you be immune to this bitter water that brings a curse.
But if you have gone astray while under your husband's authority and have defiled yourself and lain carnally with a man other than your husband',
and the priest shall have the woman swear under the oath of the curse, ‘then may the LORD make you an attested curse among your people by making your thigh shrivel and your belly swell.

May this water that brings a curse enter your stomach and cause your belly to swell and your thigh to shrivel.’ 
Then the woman is to say, ‘Amen, Amen.’
And the priest shall write these curses on a scroll and wash them off into the bitter water.
He is to have the woman drink the bitter water that brings a curse, and it will enter her and cause her bitter suffering.
The priest shall take from her hand the grain offering for jealousy, wave it before the LORD, and bring it to the altar.
Then the priest is to take a handful of the grain offering as a memorial portion and burn it on the altar; after that he is to have the woman drink the water.

When he has made her drink the water, if she has defiled herself and been unfaithful to her husband, then the water that brings a curse will enter her and cause bitter suffering; her belly will swell, her thigh will shrivel, and she will become accursed among her people.
But if the woman has not defiled herself and is clean, she will be unaffected and able to conceive children.
This is the law of jealousy when a wife goes astray and defiles herself while under her husband's authority,
or when a feeling of jealousy comes over a husband and he suspects his wife. He is to have the woman stand before the LORD, and the priest is to apply to her this entire law.
The husband will be free from guilt, but the woman shall bear her iniquity.”

unfaithful.gloss: Unfaithfulness, trespass, or treachery. Elsewhere in Scripture this word describes covenant betrayal against the LORD Himself, so a wrong done against another person is here treated as unfaithfulness against God, not merely a private matter between neighbors.

jealousy.gloss: Jealousy or zeal, a strong emotion that can be destructive suspicion or righteous zeal depending on context. Here it describes a husband's suspicion, which the law refuses to let stand as proof; only God's own verdict, not human accusation, settles the matter.

basic.context: As Israel prepares to break camp from Sinai, God gives instructions to guard the purity of the community He dwells among: the ceremonially unclean must be sent outside the camp, wrongs against another person must be confessed and repaid with interest, and a wife secretly suspected of unfaithfulness undergoes a solemn ritual that leaves the verdict entirely in God's hands.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a specific set of case laws for ancient Israel's camp life and legal system, not a repeatable ceremony for today. Its most difficult section, the bitter water ordeal, must be read carefully: it protected an accused woman from vigilante justice or an accuser's mere assumption, since only God, not a human court or the husband himself, determined guilt.
basic.originalLanguages: The law addresses a husband's feeling of qinah, jealousy, and a wife's possible maal, unfaithfulness or trespass, framing marital betrayal as ultimately a sin against God Himself, not merely against a spouse, since the sanctuary dust and holy water at the center of the ritual involve the LORD directly as judge.
basic.typology: The sending of the unclean outside the camp anticipates the greater cleansing accomplished by Christ, who bore uncleanness outside the camp at the cross, Hebrews 13:12-13, so that His people could be brought fully into God's presence rather than kept at a distance.
basic.christ: Where this law required confession and repayment with an added fifth for wrongs done, and left hidden unfaithfulness to God's own judgment, Christ both makes atonement for sin and searches the hidden heart, John 2:24-25, fulfilling the pattern of a God who deals honestly with both open guilt and secret betrayal.
basic.god: God cares about the purity of the community He dwells among, in matters of health, of interpersonal wrong, and of hidden marital faithfulness. He is not only just in dealing with obvious sin but also entrusts what cannot be proven by human courts entirely to His own righteous knowledge and judgment.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 13:12-13 speaks of Jesus suffering outside the camp to sanctify His people. Leviticus 6:1-7 parallels the restitution plus a fifth required here for wrongs against a neighbor. 1 Corinthians 4:5 says the Lord will bring hidden things to light, echoing the jealousy law's confidence in God's just verdict.
basic.oneStory: From the earliest days of the wilderness camp, God cares about purity that is physical, relational, and hidden, providing means of cleansing, restitution, and just verdict even where human courts cannot see. That concern for holiness in every dimension of life finds its answer in Christ, who cleanses what is unclean and judges what is hidden.

context.literary: This chapter groups three distinct but related case laws under a single theme, camp purity, forming a transition between the organizational material of chapters 1 to 4 and the Nazirite and priestly blessing material of chapter 6.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern legal codes contain parallel ordeals for suspected adultery, but Israel's version is distinctive in placing the verdict entirely with the LORD rather than with human magic, omens, or a priest's independent judgment, and it protects the woman from being condemned merely on accusation.
context.canonical: The principle of confession and restitution echoes Leviticus 6, and the concern for hidden sin known fully to God alone anticipates Psalm 139's confession that no darkness can hide anything from the LORD.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To protect the purity of the camp where God dwells by removing what defiles, requiring honest confession and repayment for wrongs, and providing a God-centered resolution for suspected unfaithfulness that could not otherwise be proven.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes specific case laws for ancient Israel's wilderness community; it does not prescribe that the bitter water ordeal or the exact restitution formula be practiced today. Its abiding principles are that sin against others requires honest confession and repair, and that God alone sees and judges what is hidden.

debates.question: Was the law of jealousy, verses 11 to 31, oppressive to women, or protective?

views.label: A protective legal safeguard for the accused woman
views.case: Unlike surrounding cultures where a jealous husband could act on suspicion alone, this law forces the matter before the LORD and the priest, and a woman who is innocent is explicitly vindicated and even blessed with children, verse 28, rather than punished on mere accusation.

views.label: A law reflecting its patriarchal setting
views.case: Others note the law applies only to a husband's suspicion of his wife, with no parallel ordeal described for a wife who suspects her husband, reflecting the unequal legal position of women in that culture.

debates.bearing: Both views can be held together: the law restrains and protects against unproven accusation more than the culture around it did, while it also reflects its ancient setting rather than establishing a timeless procedure for marital suspicion today.

study.originalLanguages: The husband's feeling is described as qinah, a spirit of jealousy, while the wife's alleged sin is maal, unfaithfulness or trespass, a word elsewhere describing covenant betrayal against the LORD Himself, showing that marital faithfulness is treated as inseparable from faithfulness to God. The ritual's use of sanctuary dust and holy water keeps the verdict entirely within God's own domain rather than human magic.

typology.type: The unclean sent outside the camp
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who suffered outside the camp to sanctify His people through His own blood, Hebrews 13:12-13.

typology.type: Hidden sin brought before the LORD for judgment
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who knows what is in man, John 2:24-25, and before whom all hidden things will be disclosed, 1 Corinthians 4:5.

study.christ: The law's insistence that hidden unfaithfulness be entrusted to God's own knowledge and verdict, rather than human suspicion, anticipates Christ, who did not need anyone to testify about man, for He knew what was in man, John 2:25, and who will one day bring to light the hidden things of darkness and disclose the purposes of the heart, 1 Corinthians 4:5.
study.god: God is concerned with purity that runs deeper than appearances: physical uncleanness, unpaid debts of wrong, and hidden marital betrayal all matter to Him because He dwells among His people. Yet He is also protective of the vulnerable, providing a fair process rather than leaving an accused woman to suspicion alone.

crossReferences.note: Describes Jesus suffering outside the camp to sanctify His people, echoing the unclean sent outside the camp here.
Parallels the restitution plus a fifth required for wrongs against a neighbor in this chapter.
Says the Lord will bring hidden things to light, echoing this chapter's confidence that God alone can judge what cannot be proven.
Confesses that God knows every hidden thought and way, the deeper reality behind the jealousy ordeal's trust in His judgment.

study.oneStory: From the earliest days of the wilderness camp, God cares about purity that is physical, relational, and hidden, providing means of cleansing, restitution, and a just verdict even where human courts cannot see. That concern for holiness in every dimension of life finds its answer in Christ, who cleanses what is unclean and judges rightly what is hidden.

quiz.q: Who was to be sent outside the camp according to the opening instructions of this chapter?

options.text: Anyone with a skin disease, anyone with a bodily discharge, and anyone defiled by contact with a dead body.
options.note: Verse 2 lists these three conditions exactly.

options.text: Only foreigners living among Israel.
options.note: The instruction applies to Israelites, male and female alike, verse 3, not specifically foreigners.

options.text: Anyone who disagreed with Moses.
options.note: This is not mentioned anywhere in the chapter.

options.text: No one; the camp had no such requirement.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 give this requirement explicitly.

quiz.q: What did the law require of someone who sinned against another person and was found guilty?

options.text: Full confession and full restitution of the wrong, plus an additional fifth of its value.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 state this requirement exactly.

options.text: Only a verbal apology, without any repayment.
options.note: The text explicitly requires repayment, not merely an apology.

options.text: Permanent exile from the camp.
options.note: This is not the consequence stated for this offense.

options.text: No requirement is given.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 state the requirement plainly.

quiz.q: What was the priest to do if a husband suspected his wife of unfaithfulness but had no witness or proof?

options.text: Bring her before the LORD and follow the prescribed grain offering and water ritual, letting God reveal the truth.
options.note: Verses 12 to 28 describe this entire procedure.

options.text: Immediately have her stoned on the husband's word alone.
options.note: The text requires a whole process before the LORD, not immediate punishment on accusation.

options.text: Simply believe the husband's accusation without any further process.
options.note: An entire ritual process is prescribed instead of accepting the accusation alone.

options.text: The text gives no procedure for this situation.
options.note: Verses 12 to 28 describe the procedure in detail.

quiz.q: What happened to a woman who underwent the ritual and was innocent?

options.text: She was unaffected by the water and would be able to conceive children.
options.note: Verse 28 states this outcome for an innocent woman.

options.text: She was still exiled from the camp regardless of her innocence.
options.note: This contradicts the explicit vindication described in verse 28.

options.text: She had to pay a fine to her husband even if found innocent.
options.note: No such fine is mentioned in the text.

options.text: The text does not describe the outcome for an innocent woman.
options.note: Verse 28 describes this outcome directly.

quiz.q: According to the chapter's closing summary, who bears the guilt if the ritual reveals the wife was truly unfaithful?

options.text: The husband is free from guilt in bringing the matter before the LORD, but the guilty wife bears her own iniquity.
options.note: Verses 30 to 31 state this division of responsibility exactly.

options.text: The priest bears the guilt for administering the ritual, regardless of outcome.
options.note: No such statement about the priest's guilt appears in the text.

options.text: Guilt is shared equally between husband and wife regardless of the outcome.
options.note: Verse 31 assigns guilt specifically, not equally regardless of outcome.

options.text: The text assigns no responsibility for guilt at all.
options.note: Verse 31 addresses this directly.

questions: God required that even the ceremonially unclean be sent outside the camp so His dwelling among His people would not be defiled. What areas of your life do you keep too close to what you know displeases God?
Confession of sin was to be followed by real restitution, not just words. Is there a wrong you have named to God but never actually made right with the person you wronged?

The jealousy law entrusted what could not be proven to God's own judgment rather than human suspicion. Where are you carrying suspicion or an unresolved accusation that you need to hand over to God rather than try to resolve on your own?

An innocent woman under this law was explicitly vindicated, not merely tolerated. How does knowing God actively vindicates the innocent shape how you wait for justice in situations where you have been wrongly accused?

This whole chapter assumes that God's presence in the camp makes purity, honesty, and faithfulness matter in every corner of life, public and private. What hidden area of your life would look different if you truly believed God was as present there as anywhere else?

translationNotes.note: Acts unfaithfully against the LORD by committing any sin against another shows the Hebrew maal treats wrongs against a neighbor as simultaneously an offense against God.

The bitter water that brings a curse combines holy water with dust from the tabernacle floor and ink washed from a written curse, making the ritual itself, not human accusation, the instrument that would reveal guilt or innocence.
The woman shall bear her iniquity clarifies that the ritual's outcome, not the husband's initial suspicion, determines guilt; an innocent woman bears nothing.
